narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and LSAT score of 35, compared to the usual 36.7. Your Year 1 school decile ranking of 4 also trails the average of about 5.7, reflecting grades that placed you in the middle of your peers. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 49.9% chance that you would fail the bar—high enough to tip the decision toward rejecting your application, as the school prioritizes those predicted to pass on the first try. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on just a few key factors from your record that carried the most weight. Your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the model toward seeing you as likely to pass despite other concerns. However, your Year 1 decile ranking pulled significantly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a higher risk of bar failure based on early academic struggles relative to peers. Your undergraduate GPA reinforced that concern as the third-biggest factor, with its lower-than-average value nudging the prediction further toward failure. In essence, while your LSAT showed real promise, your earlier grades created enough doubt to outweigh it when stacked against typical successful applicants, who often have stronger deciles around 5-6 and GPAs closer to 3.2. This close call—nearly 50%—means you're right on the edge compared to students whose profiles predict under 30% failure risk and get approved. To turn things around, focus on boosting your academic standing, perhaps through a post-bac program or retaking the LSAT to hit 37 or higher, and gather strong letters highlighting your resilience as a full-time student from a middle-income background. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
